title: End of module: Hypothesis Testing for a Binomial Proportion

questions.text: In a hypothesis test for a population proportion $p$, the null hypothesis $H_0$ is always written as…
choices: $p = p_0$ for a specific numerical value $p_0$
$p < p_0$ for a specific numerical value $p_0$
$p \ne p_0$ for a specific numerical value $p_0$
whatever value makes the sample data most likely
questions.explanation: $H_0$ is always a statement of equality, $H_0: p = p_0$, where $p_0$ is the specific value being tested (e.g. a manufacturer's claim). The direction of any inequality belongs to $H_1$, not $H_0$, and $H_0$ is fixed before the data is seen, not chosen to fit it.

questions.text: A researcher wants to test whether a coin is biased, without assuming a direction of bias, at the $5\%$ level. Which alternative hypothesis is correct?
questions.explanation: "Biased, without assuming a direction" means the true proportion could differ from $0.5$ in either direction, so this is a two-tailed test: $H_1: p \ne 0.5$. A one-tailed alternative ($p<0.5$ or $p>0.5$) would only be correct if the question specified a direction of bias.

questions.text: A courier company claims $40\%$ of parcels are delivered within one hour ($H_0: p = 0.4$). A manager tests $H_1: p < 0.4$ at the $5\%$ significance level using a sample of $n = 20$ parcels. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(20, 0.4)$, with $P(X \le 3) = 0.0160$ and $P(X \le 4) = 0.0510$. What is the largest value $c$ (the upper end of the critical region $X \le c$) such that $P(X \le c) \le 0.05$?
questions.explanation: We need the largest $c$ with $P(X \le c) \le 0.05$. Since $P(X \le 3) = 0.0160 \le 0.05$ but $P(X \le 4) = 0.0510 > 0.05$, the critical region is $X \le 3$, so $c = 3$ (actual significance level $1.60\%$).

questions.text: A call centre claims $50\%$ of calls are resolved on the first attempt ($H_0: p = 0.5$). A manager tests $H_1: p > 0.5$ at the $5\%$ level with $n = 25$ calls. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(25, 0.5)$, with $P(X \ge 18) = 0.0216$ and $P(X \ge 17) = 0.0539$. What is the smallest value $c$ (the lower end of the critical region $X \ge c$) such that $P(X \ge c) \le 0.05$?
questions.explanation: We need the smallest $c$ with $P(X \ge c) \le 0.05$. Since $P(X \ge 18) = 0.0216 \le 0.05$ but $P(X \ge 17) = 0.0539 > 0.05$, the critical region is $X \ge 18$, so $c = 18$ (actual significance level $2.16\%$).

questions.text: A quality inspector tests $H_0: p = 0.1$ against $H_1: p > 0.1$ for a defect rate. The sample data leads to rejecting $H_0$, concluding the defect rate has risen — but in fact the true defect rate is still exactly $0.1$. What kind of error is this?
choices: Type I error
Type II error
No error — the conclusion is correct by definition
This cannot be classified as an error
questions.explanation: This is a **Type I error**: rejecting a true $H_0$. The null hypothesis ($p = 0.1$) was actually correct, but the test's decision rejected it — a false alarm. By construction $P(\text{Type I error}) \le \alpha$, the significance level.

questions.text: A vaccine trial tests $H_0: p = 0.8$ against $H_1: p < 0.8$. In fact the vaccine's true effectiveness is only $p = 0.65$, but the sample data does not fall in the critical region, so $H_0$ is not rejected. What kind of error has occurred?
choices: Type I error
Type II error
No error — failing to reject $H_0$ is always the safe choice
This cannot be classified as an error
questions.explanation: This is a **Type II error**: failing to reject a false $H_0$. The true proportion ($p = 0.65$) means $H_0: p = 0.8$ is actually false, but the test failed to detect this and did not reject $H_0$ — a missed detection.

questions.text: A factory claims $20\%$ of items from a process are irregular ($H_0: p = 0.2$). An inspector tests $H_1: p > 0.2$ at the $1\%$ level with $n = 30$ items. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(30, 0.2)$, with $P(X \ge 11) = 0.0256$ and $P(X \ge 12) = 0.0095$. What is the smallest value $c$ such that $P(X \ge c) \le 0.01$?
questions.explanation: We need the smallest $c$ with $P(X \ge c) \le 0.01$. Since $P(X \ge 11) = 0.0256 > 0.01$ but $P(X \ge 12) = 0.0095 \le 0.01$, the critical region is $X \ge 12$, so $c = 12$ (actual significance level $0.95\%$, the closest achievable value not exceeding $1\%$).

questions.text: A test of $H_0: p = 0.5$ against $H_1: p \ne 0.5$ is carried out at the $10\%$ level with $n = 10$. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(10, 0.5)$, with $P(X \le 1) = 0.0107$, $P(X \le 2) = 0.0547$, $P(X \ge 8) = 0.0547$ and $P(X \ge 9) = 0.0107$. Splitting $10\%$ equally between the two tails ($5\%$ each), which is the correct two-tailed critical region?
choices: $X \le 1$ or $X \ge 9$
$X \le 2$ or $X \ge 8$
$X \le 1$ or $X \ge 8$
$X \le 2$ or $X \ge 9$
questions.explanation: Each tail must have probability $\le 0.05$. Lower tail: $P(X \le 1) = 0.0107 \le 0.05$ but $P(X \le 2) = 0.0547 > 0.05$, so the lower critical region is $X \le 1$. Upper tail: $P(X \ge 9) = 0.0107 \le 0.05$ but $P(X \ge 8) = 0.0547 > 0.05$, so the upper critical region is $X \ge 9$. Overall: $X \le 1$ or $X \ge 9$ (actual significance level $2.14\%$).

questions.text: A gardener claims $60\%$ of a certain plant's seeds germinate ($H_0: p = 0.6$). A rival tests $H_1: p < 0.6$ at the $5\%$ level with $n = 12$ seeds. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(12, 0.6)$. Given $P(X = 0) = 0.00002$, $P(X = 1) = 0.00032$ and $P(X = 2) = 0.00248$, find the p-value for an observed result of $x = 2$ seeds germinating, i.e. $P(X \le 2)$, to 4 decimal places.
questions.explanation: The p-value for a lower-tail test with observed value $x = 2$ is $P(X \le 2) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + P(X=2) = 0.00002 + 0.00032 + 0.00248 = 0.0028$. Since $0.0028 \le 0.05$, this result would be significant at the $5\%$ level — there would be sufficient evidence to reject $H_0$ and conclude the germination rate is below $60\%$.

questions.text: A charity claims $10\%$ of mailed leaflets result in a donation ($H_0: p = 0.1$). A new leaflet design is tested against $H_1: p > 0.1$ at the $5\%$ level with $n = 50$ leaflets. Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(50, 0.1)$, and the critical region is found to be $X \ge 10$ (since $P(X \ge 10) = 0.0245 \le 0.05$ but $P(X \ge 9) = 0.0579 > 0.05$). In a trial, $9$ leaflets result in a donation. What is the correct conclusion?
choices: Reject $H_0$: there is sufficient evidence the new design increases the donation rate above $10\%$
Do not reject $H_0$: there is insufficient evidence the new design increases the donation rate above $10\%$
Accept $H_0$ as proven true: the donation rate is exactly $10\%$
The test is inconclusive and no conclusion can be drawn
questions.explanation: The critical region is $X \ge 10$. The observed value $x = 9$ does **not** lie in this region (since $9 < 10$), so we do not reject $H_0$. Conclusion in context: there is insufficient evidence, at the $5\%$ level, that the new leaflet design increases the donation rate above $10\%$. (Note: 'accept $H_0$ as proven' is never valid phrasing — a test can only fail to find evidence against $H_0$.)
